track_name: Data Analyst / Data Scientist / AI/ML Engineer

semesters.focus: Programming Foundations + DSA Basics + Git + Web Introduction
semesters.objective: By the end of II‑I, every student can write basic programs in Python/Java/C, understand fundamental DSA, use Git/GitHub, and have a taste of HTML/CSS/JS.

details.title: Syntax, variables, conditionals, loops, functions, I/O.
Basic OOP: classes, objects, methods, constructors.
Error handling, debugging practices.

modules.brief: Fundamentals of programming in Python including syntax, conditionals, functions, object-oriented concepts, and debugging.

topics.title: Time and space complexity intuition (Big‑O at a high level).
Simple problems from platforms (2 h of guided practice).

modules.brief: Introduction to basic data structures (arrays, strings), recursion ideas, and Big-O runtime analysis.

topics.title: Git basics: init, add, commit, log, branch, merge.
Remote repositories: clone, push, pull, pull request workflow.
Classroom activity: students push a small programming assignment repo.

modules.brief: Version control essentials with Git, remote repositories on GitHub, and pull request workflows.

topics.title: HTML fundamentals: structure, forms, common tags.
CSS essentials: selectors, box model, basic layout.
JavaScript intro: variables, functions, DOM basics, events.

modules.brief: Foundational web skills: HTML5 markup structure, CSS selectors/layouts, and basic JavaScript page interactivity.

topics.title: SDLC models; emphasis on Agile/Scrum basics (roles, ceremonies, user stories).
Versioning + issue tracking (GitHub Issues, basic Kanban). Deliverables by end of II‑I:
A GitHub profile with at least 2 small coding repos and 1 static portfolio page.

modules.brief: Software development lifecycles, Agile/Scrum framework roles, issue tracking, and versioning standards.

semesters.post_assessment: Baseline coding test to decide who leans to SE/FSD vs Data vs Cloud/DevOps/Security.

semesters.focus: Strengthen DSA + Teach SQL + Start Track‑oriented modules
semesters.split_info: Common Modules - 24 h + Track Specific Modules - 24 h

details.title: Data structures: arrays, linked lists, stacks, queues, hash maps, basic trees.
Algorithms: searching, sorting, introduction to recursion and greedy ideas.
4–6 h of problem‑solving labs (LeetCode/GFG‑style curated sets).
ER modeling basics.
SQL: CREATE, SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, WHERE, JOINS, GROUP BY, HAVING.
Simple lab: design and query a mini student‑course database.

modules.brief: Core semester modules covering data structures & algorithms (DSA) and relational database management (SQL).

details.title: Data Handling with Pandas & NumPy – 7 h
Loading datasets from CSV/Excel into Pandas.
Inspecting data: head, info, describe, dtypes.
Cleaning basics: handling missing values (drop/fill), removing duplicates.
Filtering rows, selecting columns, sorting.
GroupBy operations for simple aggregations (sum, mean, count).
Basic NumPy arrays for numeric operations.
Basic Descriptive Statistics – 2 h
Mean, median, mode, min, max, standard deviation.
Simple frequency distributions and basic visual checks (histogram/boxplot via Pandas/Matplotlib).
AI‑Assisted Data Work & Prompting (1 h – awareness + demo)
Explain how modern data analysts use AI tools to:

details.text: Generate Python snippets for data cleaning/EDA.
Suggest transformations or feature ideas, given a schema description.

details.title: Introduce basic prompt engineering for data tasks:

details.text: How to clearly describe a dataset, the goal, and expected output when asking an AI assistant.

details.title: Instructor demonstration:

details.text: Show a small CSV structure and ask an AI assistant for code to “clean and summarize” it, then review the code for correctness and adjust as needed. Expected Outcome: Students can manually perform core data cleaning and EDA in Python and also understand how to use AI assistants as helpers (not replacements) for routine data tasks.

details.title: Core Excel Analytics – 4 h
Data cleaning basics in Excel: remove duplicates, text-to-columns, basic formatting.
Lookup functions: VLOOKUP, XLOOKUP, INDEX‑MATCH (conceptually).
Pivot tables: building pivots, grouping, basic calculated fields.
Simple data modeling: arranging raw data into analyzable tables.
Excel + AI Integration Awareness – 2 h
Explain that modern Excel/office tools increasingly include AI helpers (e.g., Copilot, formula suggestions, automated insights).
Demonstrate (if available) or conceptually explain:

details.text: Using AI to generate formulas, suggest pivot configurations, or summarize trends in a table.

details.title: Stress good practice:

details.text: Always validate AI‑suggested formulas and summaries against the underlying data. Expected Outcome: Students can analyse data with Excel and understand that AI‑enhanced Excel can speed up work, but still requires human verification and understanding of pivots/lookups.

topics.title: Introduction to Data Visualization Tools – 8 h

details.title: Visualization Basics – 3 h
Good chart practices: choosing chart types for comparisons, trends, distributions.
Linking visuals to questions: “What decision should this chart support?”
Hands‑on with Power BI or Tableau – 4 h
Importing data from CSV/Excel.
Creating basic visuals: bar, line, pie/donut, tables.
Building a simple interactive dashboard with filters/slicers.
AI in BI Tools – 1 h (awareness + guided example)
Many BI tools now offer AI features such as:

details.text: “Explain this visual” / “Quick insights”,
Natural language queries (“show sales by region last year”).

details.title: Demonstrate (if your license supports it) or conceptually explain:

details.text: Asking the BI tool to generate insights or charts with natural language, and then verifying if those insights match the underlying data.

details.title: Connect to AI analyst roles:

details.text: Data analysts increasingly need to interpret AI-suggested insights, not just produce charts. Expected Outcome: Students gain basic dashboarding skills and understand that BI tools now come with AI‑driven insight and NLQ features, which they must use critically and responsibly.

modules.brief: Introduction to specialization domains: software engineering, DevOps pipelines, or security concepts.

pre_requisites: (Python for Data - 10h + Excel for Analytics - 6h + Data Visualization Tools - 8h) Pre-requisites: Students should be comfortable with basic programming (ideally some Python at “variables–loops–lists” level), school‑level statistics (mean/median, percentages), and basic spreadsheet use (entering/sorting data, simple formulas) so this module can focus on using Python, Excel, and BI tools for real data cleaning, analysis, and visualization with AI‑assisted support.

semesters.description: Statistics and Mathematics for Data - 14h + Data Analytics with Python - 14h + SQL for Analytics - 10h + Introduction to Machine Learning - 10h)
pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable with Python basics (variables, loops, lists, simple functions),
introductory statistics (mean/median, variance, basic probability ideas), core SQL
(SELECT/FROM/WHERE, simple JOIN + GROUP BY), and basic spreadsheet/BI familiarity,
so this semester can focus on formal stats for data, serious EDA with Python, intermediate
analytical SQL, and first ML models with Scikit‑learn.

details.title: Mean, median, mode, variance, standard deviation.
Covariance, correlation (basic).
Random variables: discrete vs continuous, expectation and variance (informal level).
Common distributions (normal, binomial, Poisson – conceptual).
Sampling, sample vs population, law of large numbers (intuitive).
Confidence intervals (idea, not proofs).
Null vs alternative hypotheses, type I/II errors.
p‑value concept and basic test examples (mean difference, proportion).

topics.title: Why This Math Matters for AI & ML – 2 h (conceptual)

details.title: Connect these topics to AI/ML practice:

details.text: How distributions and variance affect model assumptions and uncertainty.
Why hypothesis testing and confidence intervals matter for A/B tests and model comparisons.

details.title: Short examples:

details.text: Evaluating whether a new ML model is significantly better than an old one.

Understanding model predictions as coming from distributions, not fixed values. Expected Outcome: Students see statistics not just as theory but as the basis of AI/ML models and evaluation, especially for Data Scientist and AI/ML Engineer roles.

modules.brief: Mathematical foundations for data science, statistics, probability, linear algebra matrices, and hypothesis tests.

details.title: Loading data, inspecting structure, checking missing values and types.
Univariate analysis: histograms, boxplots, descriptive stats.
Bivariate analysis: scatterplots, group comparisons.
Strategies for missing data (drop vs impute).
Detecting and treating outliers (visual + rule‑of‑thumb).
Basic feature transformation (log transforms, simple encodings).

topics.title: Feature Understanding & Simple Feature Engineering – 2 h

details.title: Correlation matrices and basic feature importance intuition.
Creating simple derived features (ratios, aggregates).
Explain that EDA is a critical step before ML/AI, and many tools now provide AI‑powered EDA/AutoML.
Concepts:

details.text: AI tools can auto‑summarize datasets, detect outliers, suggest transformations, and generate EDA reports.

details.title: Instructor demo (or conceptual walkthrough):

details.text: Show how an AutoML/AI EDA tool could automatically produce summary stats, plots, and highlight suspicious patterns.

details.title: Stress good practice:

details.text: Use AI EDA suggestions as starting points, but still manually interpret plots and verify that transformations make sense for the domain.
Expected Outcome: Students can run manual EDA with Python and understand the role of AI‑powered EDA/AutoML tools in speeding up, but not replacing, human-driven exploratory analysis.

modules.brief: Cleaning, manipulating, and analyzing datasets using Python data libraries (Pandas/NumPy) and plotting libraries.

details.title: Review joins (INNER/LEFT/RIGHT) with clear examples.
Common aggregation patterns (GROUP BY, HAVING).
Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, moving averages at beginner level.
Writing CTEs for readability and step‑wise analysis.
Multi‑step analytical queries using CTEs and windows.

topics.title: AI‑Assisted SQL & “AI Data Analyst” Role – 1 h (awareness + demo/description)

details.title: Explain how AI tools help analysts craft and optimize SQL queries from natural language prompts (“show sales by region per month”).
Demonstrate or describe:

details.text: Turning a business question into SQL with the help of an AI assistant, then manually reviewing and fixing the query.

details.title: Connect to AI Data Analyst roles:

details.text: Analysts increasingly use AI assistants to speed up queries, but must still understand joins, windows, and logic to check correctness.
Expected Outcome: Students write complex analytical SQL themselves and understand how AI‑driven SQL assistants can support (but not replace) their analytical reasoning.

modules.brief: Writing queries in SQL, database joins, grouping/aggregations, and database schemas.

details.title: Supervised learning basics: input features X, target y.
Regression: e.g., Linear Regression on a simple dataset.
Classification: e.g., Logistic Regression or Decision Tree on labeled data.
Train/test split and simple pipeline for training and prediction.
Overfitting vs underfitting; bias‑variance intuition.
Evaluation metrics: accuracy, precision, recall, F1, RMSE for regression.
Simple confusion matrix interpretation.

topics.title: Positioning ML within the AI/Data Science Pipeline – 1 h

details.title: Show the full AI/Data Science pipeline:

details.text: Data collection → EDA → feature engineering → ML models → evaluation → deployment & monitoring.

details.title: Clarify roles:

details.text: Data Analyst – strong on SQL, Excel/BI, descriptive analytics.
Data Scientist / AI/ML Engineer – extend this with model development, tuning, deployment, and integration into applications.

details.title: Brief mention of AutoML:

details.text: Tools that automate model selection/tuning, and why understanding fundamentals (as taught here) is still crucial to use them responsibly.

Expected Outcome: Students can build basic ML models in Scikit‑learn and see where this fits within the broader AI and Data Science lifecycle, preparing them for deeper ML and AI/ML Engineer content in III‑II and beyond.

modules.brief: Fundamentals of machine learning, training regression and classification models using Scikit-Learn.

semesters.description: (Machine Learning and AI - 18h + Data Visualization and Storytelling - 10h + Big Data and Cloud for Data - 10h + Mini Project - 10h)
pre_requisites: Students should be comfortable with Python for data work (Pandas/NumPy basics, EDA), core
statistics and probability (descriptive stats, common distributions, sampling ideas), intermediate
SQL for analytics (joins, GROUP BY, simple windows), and basic dashboarding in a BI tool, so
this semester can focus on training and evaluating ML models, advanced data storytelling, and
big‑data/cloud ML workflows with AI‑aware tooling.

topics.title: Supervised Models: Logistic, Trees, Random Forest – 10 h

details.title: Logistic Regression for binary classification (with regularization idea).
Decision Trees: splitting criteria (Gini/entropy conceptually), interpretability.
Random Forest: ensembles, variance reduction, feature importance.
Hands‑on with Scikit‑learn on 1–2 real datasets (classification focus).

topics.title: Unsupervised: k‑means & Clustering Use Cases – 3 h

details.title: k‑means clustering: idea, distance measure, inertia.
Simple applications: customer segmentation, pattern discovery.
Confusion matrix, accuracy, precision, recall, F1.
ROC curve & AUC (intuition and usage).
Train/validation/test splits and k‑fold cross‑validation for robust estimates.

topics.title: Intro to Neural Networks & Deep Learning – 2 h (high level AI view)

details.title: Basic neural network concepts: layers, weights, activation functions.
Example applications: image classification, text classification.
Demo or conceptual overview using Keras (simple feedforward net on a tiny dataset).

Position NN as the core of many modern AI systems (CV, NLP, LLMs). Expected Outcome: Students can train and evaluate tree‑based and linear models, plus a simple NN, and understand how these form the algorithmic core of many AI applications.

modules.brief: Expanding machine learning skills into deep learning, neural networks, computer vision, and NLP.

topics.title: Dashboard Building with Power BI/Tableau – 6 h

details.title: Connect to a realistic dataset (e.g., sales, HR, ed‑tech).
Design interactive dashboards: filters/slicers, drill‑downs, key KPI cards.
Best practices: layout, visual hierarchy, avoiding clutter.
Story arc: context → insight → recommendation.
Choosing visuals that support the narrative (trend vs distribution vs comparison).
Adding annotations, tooltips, and narrative text to explain insights.

topics.title: AI Features in BI & Communicating AI Model Results – 1 h

details.title: Many BI tools offer AI capabilities:

details.text: “Quick insights”, “Explain this visual”, natural language query (NLQ).

details.title: Brief demo or explanation:

details.text: Use an AI/NLQ feature (if available) to ask a question about the data and generate visual/insights; discuss accuracy and limitations.

details.title: Connecting to ML:

details.text: Show how model outputs (predictions, segment labels, risk scores) can be visualized and communicated to business stakeholders via dashboards.

Expected Outcome: Students can create clear, story‑driven dashboards and understand how to present AI/ML model results in a way decision‑makers can understand, including critically using AI features in BI tools.

modules.brief: Building interactive dashboards and using data visualization tools (Tableau/Power BI) to tell compelling stories.

details.title: Why big data: volume, velocity, variety.
Hadoop (HDFS, MapReduce idea) and Spark basics: distributed dataframes, transformations vs actions.
Concept of data lakes and lakehouses.
Overview of Azure ML / AWS SageMaker / GCP AI Platform (choose one for examples):

details.text: Managed notebooks, training jobs, and model deployment endpoints.

details.title: Show typical workflow:

details.text: Upload data → notebook/AutoML → train model → deploy as API.

topics.title: MLOps Touchpoint & AI in Production – 2 h (conceptual)

details.title: Introduce MLOps concepts at a high level:

details.text: Versioning data/models, deploying models, monitoring drift and performance.

details.title: Explain how cloud platforms support MLOps:

details.text: Pipelines, model registry, automated retraining.

details.title: Link back to previous topics:

details.text: Their ML models and EDA work in class are the building blocks; in industry, the same ideas scale on tools like Spark + SageMaker/Azure ML.
Expected Outcome: Students understand the big‑picture of AI/ML in the cloud, and how big data frameworks + managed ML services are used to scale and operationalize AI systems.

modules.brief: Introduction to big data processing frameworks (Hadoop/Spark) and cloud data warehousing models.

details.title: Use a real‑world dataset (internal or public).
Deliverables:

details.text: Cleaned dataset with documented transformations.
SQL/Excel/Python-based analysis answering 3–5 clear business questions.
Power BI/Tableau dashboard + 1–2 pages of narrative explaining insights and recommendations.

details.title: AI‑aware element:

details.text: At least one section discussing how AI features in BI tools or AI assistants could extend their analysis (e.g., automated anomaly detection, forecast suggestions).

details.title: Train at least one supervised model (classification/regression) or a clustering model on a real‑world dataset.
Deliverables:

details.text: Clearly defined problem (target variable, business context).
EDA summary, chosen features, model training with Scikit‑learn.
Evaluation using appropriate metrics (e.g., ROC/AUC, F1, RMSE) and cross‑validation.

details.title: Short note on possible next steps in MLOps (deployment, monitoring).

topics.title: Explicit AI Integration / Reflection (common to both options)

details.title: Students must include a short “AI Tools & Automation Reflection” (~½–1 page):

details.text: Where could AI tools (AutoML, AI EDA, BI AI features, AI code assistants) help in their project?
Did they use any, and if yes, how did they validate their outputs?

details.title: How would they scale this project in a real organization using cloud big data + managed ML services introduced earlier?

modules.brief: Applying semester concepts to build, document, and present a functional technical project prototype.
Preparing for campus placements with company-specific mock tests, technical aptitude drills, and MCQ reviews.
Improving placement readiness with professional resumes, mock interviews, and group discussion drills.
